text.title: market-sizing-with-ai Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for market-sizing-with-ai. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Always run both top-down and bottom-up estimates; accept the result only when they are within 2-3x of each other.
rule.rationale: A single-path estimate cannot self-validate. When both independent models converge, confidence increases; divergence signals a data or assumption problem that must be resolved before presenting numbers.

rule.statement: Document every assumption explicitly with a source or a flag ("estimate, unvalidated"); never let an undocumented assumption enter an investor deck.
rule.rationale: Investors will ask the source for every number. If the answer is "we assumed it," the credibility of the entire model collapses. Explicit flags give the presenter a defensible answer.

rule.statement: Assign a confidence rating (H/M/L) to every data point: H = multiple independent sources agree; M = single credible source or partial agreement; L = single low-quality source or paywalled.
rule.rationale: Confidence ratings force honest calibration and let stakeholders assess which inputs to probe further.

rule.statement: Refresh market size estimates at minimum annually; flag any data point older than 18 months in fast-moving sectors as potentially stale.
rule.rationale: Market data is point-in-time. AI tools do not flag staleness automatically; the researcher must enforce the policy.

rule.statement: Run a two-pass workflow: Haiku fetches data from report summaries; Sonnet builds both top-down and bottom-up models in parallel; Opus triangulates, stress-tests assumptions, and flags low-confidence inputs.
rule.rationale: Model specialization reduces cost and improves quality: data retrieval does not need reasoning; triangulation and stress-testing do.

rule.statement: Require a human checkpoint before any market size figure is presented externally; agent output is directional, not quotable.
rule.rationale: Agents frequently omit publication years and may reuse the same underlying source for both top-down and bottom-up paths, producing false triangulation confidence.

rule.statement: Require the agent to state the publication year of every market data point; reject outputs where year is missing.
rule.rationale: Agents routinely omit data vintage. A 2021 market size presented as current can be off by a factor of 2-3x in fast-moving sectors.

rule.statement: Add "if no credible source found, return NULL" to every data-fetch prompt; never accept a plausible-sounding number without a URL.
rule.rationale: Haiku and Sonnet will invent figures when no real data is available; the NULL instruction forces an honest failure mode instead of a fabricated answer.

rule.statement: For sensitivity analysis, require the agent to vary one assumption at a time while holding others fixed; reject outputs that vary multiple simultaneously.
rule.rationale: Varying multiple assumptions simultaneously produces noise, not insight. Single-variable sensitivity analysis isolates the drivers of variance.

rule.statement: Always run both top-down and bottom-up; accept the result only when they are within 2-3x of each other.
rule.rationale: Convergence increases confidence; divergence requires investigation.

rule.statement: Document every assumption explicitly — "ICP = 500K companies" must cite a source or be flagged as an estimate.
rule.rationale: Traceability enables stakeholders to evaluate and update assumptions later.

rule.statement: Use Perplexity Pro Search over ChatGPT for market data — Perplexity cites sources; ChatGPT often fabricates figures.
rule.rationale: Source attribution is critical for credibility and auditability.

rule.statement: Include confidence ratings (H/M/L) per data point; investors will ask, and it forces honest calibration.
rule.rationale: Explicit confidence enables risk assessment and follow-up research priorities.

rule.statement: For SOM, use realistic 3-5 year capture rates based on comparable early-stage companies, not wishful thinking.
rule.rationale: Inflated SOM undermines credibility; benchmarking against comparable companies grounds the estimate.

rule.statement: Refresh quarterly — market data older than 18 months in fast-moving sectors should be flagged as stale.
rule.rationale: Market conditions change; stale data can invalidate the entire model.

rule.statement: Agents frequently omit the year of market data; always require the agent to state the publication year explicitly.
rule.rationale: Vintage is critical; undated figures are unreliable.

rule.statement: Haiku/Sonnet will invent a plausible-sounding figure if no real data is found; add "if no credible source found, return NULL" to the prompt.
rule.rationale: Explicit instructions for failure modes prevent hallucination.

rule.statement: Top-down and bottom-up agents may both cite the same underlying Gartner/IDC estimate presented differently — triangulation is false confidence; require independent data paths.
rule.rationale: Source independence is the basis of triangulation; reused sources defeat the purpose.

rule.statement: Sensitivity analysis requires the agent to vary one assumption at a time while holding others fixed; without that instruction, agents vary multiple simultaneously and produce noise.
rule.rationale: Proper sensitivity analysis isolates drivers of variance; simultaneous variation obscures insights.

rule.statement: Human checkpoint required before any market size is presented externally; agent output is directional, not quotable.
rule.rationale: Agents are tools, not authorities; human verification is mandatory before external communication.
